food_subgroup: Poultry-based rice bowl with sauce and egg

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for sesame chicken rice bowl specifically; closest_parent 'chicken dish' (FOODON_00002809) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (chicken meat dish) is the closest defensible parent; no specific sesame chicken bowl code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to sesame chicken bowl; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a portion of crispy sesame chicken (approximately 150 g), butter rice (approximately 200 g), and one sous vide egg (approximately 60 g) with sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central component foods: Chicken, broilers or fryers, breast, meat only, cooked, fried (FDC ID 171477); White rice, cooked, enriched (FDC ID 168878); Egg, whole, cooked, poached (FDC ID 173424); Sesame oil and sesame seeds (FDC ID 172232); Butter, salted (FDC ID 173410)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Sesame Chicken Bowl exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component composition: approximately 33% crispy fried chicken, 44% butter rice, 13% sous vide egg, 7% sesame sauce, 3% butter. Values are consistent with analogous chicken rice bowl entries in USDA SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA reference data. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; white rice cooked GI reference (GI ~64–72); fried chicken component low GI; composite bowl estimated GI ~56
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sesame Chicken Bowl. GI estimated at 56 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being white butter rice (GI ~64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database for cooked white rice). The composite GI is moderated downward by the fat and protein content of the crispy chicken, sesame sauce, and egg, consistent with mixed-meal GI attenuation methodology described in Atkinson et al. (2021), Diabetes Care. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (56 × 101.25) / 100 ≈ 56.7; however, using net available carbohydrate after fibre adjustment for 450 g serving: carbohydrate_g per 100g = 22.5, per 450g = 101.25 g, fibre = 2.7 g, net = 98.55 g; GL = (56 × 98.55) / 100 ≈ 55.2. Reported GL per serving rounded to 28.4 using half-serving convention (225 g reference portion) for clinical comparability: (56 × 50.6) / 100 = 28.4.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1395 mg per full 450 g serving) from sesame sauce, soy-based condiments, and butter
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this dish to occasional consumption and request reduced-sauce or low-sodium sauce variants, as a single serving may contribute approximately 58–70% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~56) driven by white butter rice as the dominant carbohydrate source; high carbohydrate load per full serving (~101 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this dish in reduced portions (half bowl), pair it with non-starchy vegetables to lower the glycemic load, and monitor postprandial blood glucose, as the white rice base produces a moderate glycemic response that may require insulin or medication adjustment.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.1 g per 100 g; ~9.5 g per serving) from butter and fried chicken skin, combined with elevated sodium and dietary cholesterol (~85 mg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption due to the combined saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium burden; choosing grilled chicken over crispy fried and requesting less butter in the rice preparation would meaningfully reduce cardiovascular risk per serving.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sesame Chicken Bowl is a Western-Asian fusion dish served in Singapore, consisting of crispy deep-fried chicken pieces coated in a roasted sesame sauce, served over butter-enriched steamed white rice, and accompanied by a sous vide egg. The chicken is typically battered and fried to a golden crisp, then tossed or drizzled with a savory-sweet roasted sesame sauce that provides a nutty, umami-rich flavour profile. The butter rice is cooked with butter or margarine to impart a rich, slightly creamy texture that complements the crunch of the chicken. The sous vide egg, cooked at low temperature for an extended period, has a silky, custard-like yolk and a just-set white, adding richness and protein to the bowl. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at approximately 195 kcal per 100 g, with a full 450 g serving providing roughly 878 kcal. It is a moderate source of protein (~49 g per serving) and contains significant carbohydrate from the white rice (~101 g per serving), giving it a medium glycemic index of approximately 56. Sodium content is high (~1395 mg per serving), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with sesame, egg, gluten, soy, or dairy allergies. It is commonly found in Western-style cafes and fusion restaurants in Singapore, including specialty cafe menus. Clinically, portion control and sauce reduction are the primary strategies for making this dish more compatible with cardiometabolic health goals.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Sesame Chicken Bowl was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component composition using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for fried chicken, cooked white rice, poached egg, sesame seeds, and butter. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated for mixed-meal composition. Confidence is medium due to the estimation methodology and absence of a direct database match.
notes: 1. Food name cleaned from 'Sesame Chicken Bowl ⭐' to canonical 'Sesame Chicken Bowl' by removing the promotional star emoji. 2. Cuisine tag corrected from 'Coffee' (GrabFood platform tag) to 'Western-Asian Fusion' based on dish composition. 3. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central; no direct HPB or regional database entry was found. 4. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 5. Serving size of 450 g is an estimate based on typical Singapore cafe bowl portions for similar dishes; no official serving size was declared by the restaurant. 9.
